title: Webinar 85 – Launch of the Copyright Mentoring Scheme Rescheduled
content:
In this month’s webinar, members of the ALT CoOL SIG will formally launch their new peer mentoring scheme on Friday 12th June, following the postponement of this event from February. Please register for this event via the ALT Events page.

We are aware that copyright is a tricky area, and so in the spirit of sharing the burden (or love) of copyright support we are creating a peer mentoring scheme. The scheme is aimed at those looking to offer their services as a mentor, or to be mentored.

Join Jane and Chris along with Liesl Rowe and Irene Baranco Garcia to find out more about how it will operate and to have the opportunity to sign up either as a mentee or a mentor. We have also put together a webpage with more details of the scheme.

While we definitely are not aiming at romance*, we hope the scheme will be able to make a match between those working in copyright, allowing them to gain skills from each other and build expertise.

To attend, register on the ALT events website and you’ll be sent a link to the meeting.

As ever, the session will be recorded and will be available on the playlist of COOLSIG webinars recordings on the ALT You Tube channel.
snippet: In this month’s webinar, members of the ALT CoOL SIG will formally launch their new peer mentoring scheme on Friday 12th June, following the postponement of this event from February. Please register
creator: Jane Secker
sourceDescription: Decoding copyright and bringing you enlightenment

title: How CopyrightX took me by surprise…and why you should try it, too
content:
This guest post is written by Christine Daouti, who is the Copyright Support Officer at UCL. She is a previous speaker at our Icepops conference about her ‘Copyright for Humans’ game and runs an excellent UCL blog about copyright. Previously she worked in open access roles for over 20 years. She has a special interest in creating educational resources on copyright, including the UCL Copyright Essentials online module and the Copyright and your Teaching online module.

Christine writes: As many people already know, CopyrightX is a free course offered by the Harvard Law School and the Berkman Klein Center. The 12-week course runs annually from January to April and is offered in different formats. Here I discuss the ‘core’ online version taught by a Harvard Teaching Fellow; there are also affiliated versions run by copyright experts in different countries.

title: ALT CoOL SIG Annual Report 2025
content:
With apologies for the delay in publishing this report which was compiled in January 2026.

Welcome to the fifth annual report of the Copyright and Online Learning (CoOL) SIG of the Association of Learning Technology. The CoOL SIG was formed in November 2020, and this report covers the period January to December 2025. We are honoured to continue serving  as the Co-Chairs of  this group, and the report outlines the achievements of the community over the last 12 months. Once again, the CoOL SIG’s activities have centred on our Copyright and Online Learning webinars; however, we have also worked on various projects and initiatives outlined in more detail below.

We have continued to see interest in copyright and Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI), and ran two webinars on this topic, including a closed session to support the community writing a response to the government’s consultation on copyright and AI. We were delighted to run Icepops in collaboration with the University of Manchester, held at the John Rylands Research Institute and Library. 

We’ve served the group as Co-Chairs now for 5 years, and continue to be willing to support a new chair or co-chair if someone comes forward.
snippet: With apologies for the delay in publishing this report which was compiled in January 2026.
creator: Jane Secker
sourceDescription: Decoding copyright and bringing you enlightenment

title: Webinar 84 – Copyright exceptions and AI in higher education – closed session
content:
On Friday 8 May 2026 we are holding a closed webinar on copyright exceptions in UK higher education as they relate to the use of Generative AI tools. The event will be starting a little earlier than our usual webinars at 10.30am, and will run until 12pm.

The session features presentations from Lisa Bird from the University of Birmingham and Kate Vasili, Middlesex University and Chair of the UUK / Guild HE Copyright Negotiation and Advisory Committee. We will discuss a range of scenarios based on reported uses of Generative AI in higher education with various types of copyright protected content such as journal articles and books We will ask a number of questions including :

  • What copyright exceptions might apply in each given scenario? 
  • How can we make use of exceptions to help deal with issues relating to restrictive AI clauses in e-resources licences?
  • What sort of guidance should we be offering to staff and students in higher education?

    • We will be asking participants to contribute via breakout rooms and live questionnaires to build a picture of the current situation and consider next steps.

title: Copyright literacy goes to LILAC in Sheffield
content: Jane writes: We’ve been in Sheffield this week for LILAC 2026. LILAC is the UK’s information literacy conference and I’ve been part of this community since the first conference in 2005. Last year I was giving a keynote, so this year’s contribution felt a little more low key, but no less fun and impactful I hope.

title: Copyright Literacy: a small pause
content:
You may have noticed we haven’t run a webinar for a few months due to various life events taking over in the early part of this year. However, work still continues at Copyright Literacy Towers and we have been working on the Copyright Anxiety Study and will be presenting at LILAC 2026 next week. It will be great to catch up with Amanda Wakaruk who will be joining us in Sheffield and we have convened a panel entitled “It’s not Legal Advice, but is it Information Literacy“. So watch this space, the webinars will be returning soon as we are gearing up to launch our copyright mentoring scheme.

Unfortunately we won’t be able to run Icepops this year, but we will be back hopefully with something bigger and better in 2027. And if you have an idea for a webinar please do drop us a line.

title: Copyright and AI: Chris’s paper and a government report
content:
Chris writes: Last week saw the publication of two papers relating to copyright and AI. The first was the hotly awaited UK government impact assessment on Copyright and Artificial Intelligence. The second was a paper I was asked to write for Library Plus – the journal of the National Library of Korea – on copyright and libraries in the age of generative AI.

I wasn’t expecting both reports to be published on the same day, but the fact that they were provided a good opportunity to take stock not just of the public conversation about copyright and AI, but also the implication for those of us working in libraries and related memory institutions.

title: Copyright trolling survey
content:
In January, Tim Riley and Liesl Rowe gave a webinar on the Return of the Copyright Trolls, an exploration into the ongoing issue of copyright trolling. A highlight of the session was the lively discussion from participants about their own battles with copyright trolls and what they were doing to prevent it.

Tim and Liesl are keen to compile responses from other institutions to get a better sense of how copyright trolling is affecting the HE community. To start doing this, they have crafted a survey, which can be accessed here. It will remain open until 31 March 2026.

For those unfamiliar, copyright trolling is the practice of legal firms aggressively asserting the copyright of clients, sending letters alleging copyright infringement to universities. Sometimes, these requests can concern public domain material or images where the ownership is more in question. Usually, though, they take the form of letters threatening legal action which are received by senior members of university staff.

title: Webinar 85: Postponed
content:
Friday The 13th Part II – the body count continues…

After our bad luck in February, we had been hoping to avoid the curse of Friday the 13th in March. But just 28 days later, disaster has struck again. Maybe we can blame paranormal activity, but sadly we have had to take the difficult decision to postpone March’s webinar as well. Perhaps the theme of the webinar – anxiety, specifically copyright anxiety in the further education sector – was the omen we should have taken note of.

We hope to resume normal service soon, hopefully before midsummer and definitely before Halloween. We will update you here on the blog when this and February’s webinar on the launch of the copyright mentorship scheme is rescheduled. That will something to get out of bed for.

If we have to reschedule any more webinars, I think I’ll have to scream.

You’d be a psycho not to be subscribed to the blog, so you can be emailed when we post the new dates. Or you can keep an eye on the ALT events page to sign up for further webinars.

(Editor: Enough with the horror film references!)
snippet: Friday The 13th Part II – the body count continues…
creator: Kathryn D
sourceDescription: Decoding copyright and bringing you enlightenment

title: POSTPONED – Webinar 84
content:
Unfortunately, we have had to postpone Webinar 84 – Launch of the Copyright Mentorship Scheme, on Friday 13th February 2026.

Despite our best intentions to share the love of copyright for Valentine’s Day, instead we have been struck by the curse of Friday the 13th.

We hope to reschedule the webinar as soon as possible, and will post the new date and time once it’s been arranged.

But whether you are celebrating Valentine’s, Galentine’s or Palentine’s Day, everyone at Copyright Literacy sends you their love.
